explanation: The requirement specifies that the Start-up Mode should only be initiated (that is, can only be true) if and only if all of the following are satisfied: (a) the system is connected to the breathing circuit, (b) the air supply is connected, and (c) the power source is connected. Also, the Start-up Mode may only be initiated after the power button is pressed. In LTL, this can be modeled by stating that whenever StartUpMode becomes true, the following are all true now: the connections to the breathing circuit, air supply, and power are established, and (optionally) the power button was (just) pressed. However, since there is no explicit proposition for 'air supply connected,' we map 'airSupplyConnected' and 'powerConnected' as necessary, plus 'breathingCircuitConnected' (since that's more likely than 'patientConnected,' as the instruction is for use without the patient). The property uses the implication: 'StartUpMode -> (breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected & powerButton)', meaning that whenever StartUpMode is initiated, all prerequisites are satisfied at that instant. This captures the requirement that initiation of Start-up Mode is only possible when all connections (excluding the patient) and the power button press have occurred. Previous properties that didn't synchronize these conditions (e.g., just stating eventually StartUpMode after all these, or omitting the dependency) could allow invalid scenarios, such as the mode starting before all inputs are connected, which is incorrect.

The requirement specifies that Start-up Mode should only be initiated when the following conditions are met: the power button is pressed (powerButton), and the system is already connected to the breathing circuit (breathingCircuitConnected), the air supply (airSupplyConnected), and the power source (powerConnected), and that the patient is NOT connected yet (¬patientConnected). The LTL property expresses that whenever StartUpMode is entered, it is always preceded by a state where all connections (breathing circuit, air supply, power) are present, the patient is not connected, and the power button was pressed. This guarantees that system start-up can only happen after all connections except the patient are made and at the explicit action of pressing the power button.

The requirement is that Start-up Mode should only be initiated (i.e., StartUpMode should become true) by pressing the power button (powerButton), but only after the system is connected to: the breathing circuit (breathingCircuitConnected), the air supply (airSupplyConnected), and the power source (powerConnected), and without the patient being connected (i.e., not patientConnected). This is an 'enablement' condition: Start-up Mode initiation must be *triggered* by a powerButton event, and at that moment, all required connections (and specifically, *no patient connection*) are already in place. The LTL states globally: every time the powerButton is pressed, if all required are connected and the patient is *not* connected, then (next) StartUpMode will occur. This directly and faithfully captures the requirement.
output_LTL: G((powerButton & breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected & !patientConnected) -> X StartUpMode)

explanation: The requirement says that the system should only enter the Start-up Mode when it has been connected to the breathing circuit (but not to the patient), the air supply, and the power source, and then the power button is pressed. There is an atomic proposition corresponding to each needed connection: 'breathingCircuitConnected', 'airSupplyConnected', and 'powerConnected'. The proposition 'powerButton' shows the button has been pressed, and 'StartUpMode' indicates entering this mode. The proper LTL property ensures that whenever the three connections have been made and then the power button is pressed, eventually the StartUpMode is initiated. This is captured by: G((breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected & powerButton) -> F(StartUpMode)). This property ensures that at any point, if all three connections are true and the power button is pressed, then at some future point, StartUpMode holds. Previous attempts might not ensure that all conditions (connections) are present at the same time before pressing the power button, or might not require the power button press. This property captures the exact sequence by requiring all three connections and the power button together as the antecedent of the implication.

The requirement states that 'Start-up Mode' shall only be initiated by pressing the power button, and only after the system has been connected to the breathing circuit, air supply, and power source. We interpret this as requiring that StartUpMode cannot start unless the power source, air supply, and breathing circuit are all connected, and the power button has been pressed. Because no atomic proposition uniquely specifies 'air supply connection', but we have 'airSupplyConnected', 'breathingCircuitConnected', and 'powerConnected', we use these. We capture the intended meaning by stating that whenever StartUpMode is initiated, it must be the case that, at that or previously, all three prerequisite connections occurred and the power button has been pressed. Thus, we require that globally: if StartUpMode becomes true, then at some previous (or possibly the same) time, all the prerequisites (airSupplyConnected, breathingCircuitConnected, and powerConnected), and powerButton have occurred. Since LTL does not permit explicit past operators, we antecedently require that StartUpMode only ever occurs if currently all connection conditions and the power button are active. In LTL, this is expressed as: G (StartUpMode -> (airSupplyConnected & breathingCircuitConnected & powerConnected & powerButton)). This property captures the intent described in the rationale for testing and safety.

The natural language requirement specifies that 'Start-up Mode' (StartUpMode) should be initiated by pressing the power button (powerButton), but only after the system has been connected to the breathing circuit (breathingCircuitConnected), the air supply (airSupplyConnected), and the power source (powerConnected), and not to the patient (patientConnected should not be true). In temporal logic, this means the mode StartUpMode should not be activated until (U) all of these connections have been made (breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected) and patientConnected is false. Once these are true, pressing the power button (powerButton) triggers StartUpMode. Therefore, globally (G), it must hold that until all required connections are in place and the patient is NOT connected, StartUpMode is not active, and after the connections are made and patient is not connected, pressing powerButton will (eventually) activate StartUpMode. Since LTL does not express explicit triggering or sequencing tightly, the translation is:
output_LTL: G((!breathingCircuitConnected | !airSupplyConnected | !powerConnected | patientConnected) -> !StartUpMode) & G((breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected & !patientConnected & powerButton) -> F StartUpMode)

explanation: The natural language statement requires that Start-up Mode (StartUpMode) may only be initiated by pressing the power button (powerButton), but only after the system is connected to the breathing circuit (breathingCircuitConnected), air supply (airSupplyConnected), and power source (powerConnected). The LTL property below expresses that globally, if StartUpMode occurs, it is always preceded (in the previous or at the same moment) by the physical connections being present, and that StartUpMode is triggered by the power button. Since LTL expresses sequenced requirements through implication and the 'until' operator, this property states: whenever StartUpMode happens, it must be the case that (1) the connections are present and (2) the power button is pressed at that point.

The natural language specification says that Start-up Mode should be initiated by pushing the power button, but only after three things: (a) system is connected to the breathing circuit (but not the patient), (b) air supply is connected, and (c) power is supplied. In terms of available atomic propositions, 'powerButton' signals the power button has been pressed, 'StartUpMode' signals that the mode is active, 'breathingCircuitConnected' indicates connection to the circuit, 'airSupplyConnected' the air supply, and 'powerConnected' the power source. 

We capture (a)+(b)+(c) as a conjunct: (breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected), and consider the order: Start-up Mode may only activate *after* the button is pressed, and only after connections. There is an implicit requirement that before any StartUpMode can be true, the prerequisites hold and the power button is pressed.

We express this as: globally, whenever StartUpMode becomes true, it must have been preceded (in the previous step, or the same step, or possibly allow for pressing power after connections are made) by all connections present and a power button press. Minimal LTL (without past operators) typically encodes this causality by requiring globally: if StartUpMode holds, then at some point previously (or now) the conjunction and a powerButton were true. In strictly future-looking LTL, a common pattern is: it is never the case that StartUpMode occurs before all prerequisites (i.e., it's not possible for StartUpMode to become true before the conjunction and powerButton). 

Thus, we write:
G( StartUpMode -> (breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected & powerButton) )

This says: at all times, if Start-up Mode is active, then all three connections are in place and the power button has been pressed (could be same moment or persistent). This is the best approximation with the provided atomic propositions (no temporal 'before' or 'immediately after', so the system is required to keep the connections and power button true when StartUpMode starts).

To formalize the requirement, the Start-up Mode (StartUpMode) should be initiated ONLY when the system is connected to the breathing circuit (breathingCircuitConnected), to the air supply (airSupplyConnected), and to the power source (powerConnected), AND when the power button is pressed (powerButton). We interpret 'initiated by pushing the power button once the system has been connected...' as: Whenever the power button is pressed, if all three connections (breathing, air, power) are already present and the patient is NOT connected (the description says 'without connection to the patient'), then StartUpMode must be initiated. This is most commonly encoded in LTL as: It is ALWAYS the case that if the power button is pressed AND the three connections are present AND the patient is not connected, THEN eventually StartUpMode is entered.

The property is:
G((powerButton & breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected & !patientConnected) -> F StartUpMode)

This LTL means: Globally, whenever all the preconditions (including the power button press and all connections but not the patient) hold, StartUpMode must eventually be initiated. This directly captures the stated requirement. No comparison operators were used, and all named atomic propositions correspond to available dictionary entries.
output_LTL: G((powerButton & breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected & !patientConnected) -> F StartUpMode)

explanation:
The requirement is that the Start-up Mode (StartUpMode) must only be initiated by pressing the power button (powerButton) once the system has been physically connected to all required components: the breathing circuit (breathingCircuitConnected), the air supply (airSupplyConnected), and the power source (powerConnected), and with the stipulation that the patient should NOT be connected (so NOT patientConnected). 

The LTL property enforces that globally (for all times), it is NEVER permitted for StartUpMode and powerButton to be both true unless the three connections are active and the patient is not yet connected. This is done by globally requiring that whenever (StartUpMode & powerButton) holds, then at that same moment (breathingCircuitConnected & airSupplyConnected & powerConnected & !patientConnected) must also hold. 

This captures the intent, as StartUpMode cannot start by just pressing the button unless all requirements are met. Any previous LTL failing to link the power button/event synchronously with all preconditions (using implications rather than strict logical equivalence, or permitting StartUpMode w/o direct connection to these states) would be too weak and would not satisfy the specification, as it might allow StartUpMode to be entered unsafely.
